3: The debate on greenhouse gas (GHG) mitigation in agriculture is to a great extent focused on livestock, especially on ruminant production. Gerber et al. (2013) allocated 61% of global contributions of GHG emissions associated with livestock production to beef production. Regarding the importance of ruminant livestock farms in the Mediter- ranean region, mostly based on extensive grazing, there is a lack of field measurements and a low number of life cycle assessments (LCA) for livestock production in relation to the high percentage of GHG emissions associated with livestock (Aguilera et al., 2021). In addition, the esti- mation of environmental impacts in highly diverse and complex sys- items, such as extensive grassland-based livestock systems, is a complicated task (Bragaglio et al., 2018) and requires estimations for specific systems and regions.

Extensive livestock farming is always linked to pasturelands and grazing. Animal production systems based on pasture are mainly located on unsuitable lands for cultivation, forming frequently High Natural Value Systems (Díaz- Gaona et al., 2014b; Horrillo et al., 2020). The main breeds in those systems are indigenous ones, adapted to the regional environmental conditions and their pastures (Gaspar et al., 2008). Livestock grazing in these systems contributes to promoting pasture fertility and soil health, to producing high quality protein products (McAuliffe et al., 2018), and to generating ecosystem services, such as prevention of wildfires, biodiversity conservation and cultural landscapes (Aguilera et al., 2020; Díaz- Gaona et al., 2014b). Grazing management has shaped traditional landscapes, creating a huge di- versity of habitats mainly through agrosilvopastoral practices that strengthen the ecological values of the land and resource use efficiency (Díaz- Gaona et al., 2014b; Moreno and Rolo, 2019). Nevertheless, these

systems face problems related to climate change, which could lead to changes in the quality and supply of pastures, the appearance of live- stock diseases, and lower performance associated with heat stress, etc (Rivera-Ferre et al., 2016).

Life cycle assessment is an internationally accepted and standardised methodology that provides a holistic approach to calculating environ- mental impacts, including the carbon footprint (CF) of a product (Buratti et al., 2017). The low productivity of extensive livestock systems may result in higher life cycle GHG emissions per unit of product in com- parison to intensive systems (De Vries et al., 2015; McAuliffe et al., 2018), because of the higher feed efficiency and lower animal lifetimes of the intensive ones (Poore and Nemecek, 2018). However, despite LCA aims to provide a realistic view of the environmental impact of a product by including all the processes involved in the production of the raw materials used in the production system (BSI, 2011), carbon sequestra- tion (C sequestration) is not included in the majority of LCA studies comparing products from different livestock systems (Aguilera et al., 2021; Meier et al., 2015). In this regard, it is important to note that pasture-based systems are the most affected by the exclusion of C sequestration in CF calculations (Knudsen et al., 2019). The potential C sequestration in pasturelands is high (Lorenz and Lal, 2018), and it could partially or totally offset the direct and indirect livestock GHG emissions in extensive systems (Horrillo et al., 2020; Stanley et al., 2018), espe- cially with moderate grazing, which has been shown to be more efficient than low- and high-intense grazing (Byrnes et al., 2018; Jiang et al., 2020). This grazing intensity is influenced by the stocking rate (SR) and time of grazing (Abdalla al., 2018); besides that, a high SR can surpass the grassland carrying capacity, leading to requirement of supplemen- tary feed for livestock maintenance. However, there is a knowledge gap regarding to what extent C sequestration in the ecosystem can offset the GHG emissions in extensive silvopastoral systems, and usually no or only generic - information on C sequestration rates is included in CF calculations (Eldesouky et al., 2018; Horrillo et al., 2020).

Not all extensive livestock farms depend on grazing in the same proportion. Stocking rate is a farmer's decision; however, organic farming regulations ask for as much balance as possible. Organic farming systems are widely spread, and the traditional and conventional extensive systems are easily adaptable to organic specifications (Día- z-Gaona al., 2019; EC, 2009). The main differences between organic and conventional management in extensive systems are the organic bans on using GMOs, hormones, and synthetic fertilizers (EC, 2007). In terms of emissions, some authors have shown that conventional systems have lower CF than organic ones under Mediterranean conditions (Buratti et al., 2017; Pirlo and Lolli, 2019), but globally there is a lack of a consistent pattern (Poore and Nemecek, 2018; Clark and Tilman, 2017). In any case, the differences between organic and conventional CF are generally lower in extensive livestock systems (Horrillo et al., 2020) even if the organic systems have reduced performances. Given this knowledge gap, the increasing presence of organic farms and animals year by year in Europe, and particularly in Spain (MAPA, 2021) makes necessary to clarify the different levels of GHG emissions of organic and conventional systems.

The dehesa, the most extended and extensive agroforestry system in Europe (den Herder et al., 2017), covers around 4 million ha between Spain and Portugal. It is composed of herbaceous pastures, some shrubs, scattered oak trees (mostly holm and cork oaks, 20-40 trees ha and sometimes extensive crops. The main livestock types are bovine, ovine and porcine, but there are other marginal animal products such as honey or game (Rodríguez-Estévez et al., 2012); so its use results in a great diversity of products such as meat, wool, milk, some annual crops, wood and cork (Escribano et al., 2016). Its climate is Mediterranean, which is semiarid, and has soils poor in nutrients and prone to erosion (Moreno and Pulido, 2009).
On the dehesa farms, transhumant sheep herds have been greatly replaced by permanent herds of cattle, increasing at the same the SR, frequently beyond the carrying capacity of the system. This change

towards beef production has been promoted for various reasons, such as greater profitability (Hartel and Plieninger, 2014), or more subsidies from the European Common Agrarian Policy for cattle than for sheep (Moreno and Pulido, 2009). Such changes are hampering the oaks' natural replacement and the quality of the soil and pastures (Carmona et al., 2013; Moreno and Pulido, 2009).

The aim of this study is to calculate the CF of organic and conven- tional cattle dehesa farms using the LCA methodology and including C sequestration in order to assess to what extent C sequestration could offset emissions at farm scale, and if this depends on stocking rate and management (organic VS conventional).

For the first time in extensive silvopastoral systems LCA research, more than one cattle farm has been assessed in a single study, and the estimation of C sequestration has been based on direct measurement of C stocks in soil.

The main livestock types are bovine, ovine and porcine, but there are other marginal animal products such as honey or game (Rodríguez-Estévez et al., 2012); so its use results in a great diversity of products such as meat, wool, milk, some annual crops, wood and cork (Escribano et al., 2016). Its climate is Mediterranean, which is semiarid, and has soils poor in nutrients and prone to erosion (Moreno and Pulido, 2009).
On the dehesa farms, transhumant sheep herds have been greatly replaced by permanent herds of cattle, increasing at the same the SR, frequently beyond the carrying capacity of the system. This change

towards beef production has been promoted for various reasons, such as greater profitability (Hartel and Plieninger, 2014), or more subsidies from the European Common Agrarian Policy for cattle than for sheep (Moreno and Pulido, 2009). Such changes are hampering the oaks' natural replacement and the quality of the soil and pastures (Carmona et al., 2013; Moreno and Pulido, 2009).

The aim of this study is to calculate the CF of organic and conven- tional cattle dehesa farms using the LCA methodology and including C sequestration in order to assess to what extent C sequestration could offset emissions at farm scale, and if this depends on stocking rate and management (organic VS conventional).

For the first time in extensive silvopastoral systems LCA research, more than one cattle farm has been assessed in a single study, and the estimation of C sequestration has been based on direct measurement of C stocks in soil.
7: The debate on greenhouse gas (GHG) mitigation in agriculture is to a great extent focused on livestock, especially on ruminant production. Gerber et al. (2013) allocated 61% of global contributions of GHG emissions associated with livestock production to beef production. Regarding the importance of ruminant livestock farms in the Mediter- ranean region, mostly based on extensive grazing, there is a lack of field measurements and a low number of life cycle assessments (LCA) for livestock production in relation to the high percentage of GHG emissions associated with livestock (Aguilera et al., 2021). In addition, the esti- mation of environmental impacts in highly diverse and complex sys- items, such as extensive grassland-based livestock systems, is a complicated task (Bragaglio et al., 2018) and requires estimations for specific systems and regions.

Extensive livestock farming is always linked to pasturelands and grazing. Animal production systems based on pasture are mainly located on unsuitable lands for cultivation, forming frequently High Natural Value Systems (Díaz- Gaona et al., 2014b; Horrillo et al., 2020). The main breeds in those systems are indigenous ones, adapted to the regional environmental conditions and their pastures (Gaspar et al., 2008). Livestock grazing in these systems contributes to promoting pasture fertility and soil health, to producing high quality protein products (McAuliffe et al., 2018), and to generating ecosystem services, such as prevention of wildfires, biodiversity conservation and cultural landscapes (Aguilera et al., 2020; Díaz- Gaona et al., 2014b). Grazing management has shaped traditional landscapes, creating a huge di- versity of habitats mainly through agrosilvopastoral practices that strengthen the ecological values of the land and resource use efficiency (Díaz- Gaona et al., 2014b; Moreno and Rolo, 2019). Nevertheless, these

systems face problems related to climate change, which could lead to changes in the quality and supply of pastures, the appearance of live- stock diseases, and lower performance associated with heat stress, etc (Rivera-Ferre et al., 2016).

Life cycle assessment is an internationally accepted and standardised methodology that provides a holistic approach to calculating environ- mental impacts, including the carbon footprint (CF) of a product (Buratti et al., 2017). The low productivity of extensive livestock systems may result in higher life cycle GHG emissions per unit of product in com- parison to intensive systems (De Vries et al., 2015; McAuliffe et al., 2018), because of the higher feed efficiency and lower animal lifetimes of the intensive ones (Poore and Nemecek, 2018). However, despite LCA aims to provide a realistic view of the environmental impact of a product by including all the processes involved in the production of the raw materials used in the production system (BSI, 2011), carbon sequestra- tion (C sequestration) is not included in the majority of LCA studies comparing products from different livestock systems (Aguilera et al., 2021; Meier et al., 2015). In this regard, it is important to note that pasture-based systems are the most affected by the exclusion of C sequestration in CF calculations (Knudsen et al., 2019). The potential C sequestration in pasturelands is high (Lorenz and Lal, 2018), and it could partially or totally offset the direct and indirect livestock GHG emissions in extensive systems (Horrillo et al., 2020; Stanley et al., 2018), espe- cially with moderate grazing, which has been shown to be more efficient than low- and high-intense grazing (Byrnes et al., 2018; Jiang et al., 2020). This grazing intensity is influenced by the stocking rate (SR) and time of grazing (Abdalla al., 2018); besides that, a high SR can surpass the grassland carrying capacity, leading to requirement of supplemen- tary feed for livestock maintenance. However, there is a knowledge gap regarding to what extent C sequestration in the ecosystem can offset the GHG emissions in extensive silvopastoral systems, and usually no or only generic - information on C sequestration rates is included in CF calculations (Eldesouky et al., 2018; Horrillo et al., 2020).

Not all extensive livestock farms depend on grazing in the same proportion. Stocking rate is a farmer's decision; however, organic farming regulations ask for as much balance as possible. Organic farming systems are widely spread, and the traditional and conventional extensive systems are easily adaptable to organic specifications (Día- z-Gaona al., 2019; EC, 2009). The main differences between organic and conventional management in extensive systems are the organic bans on using GMOs, hormones, and synthetic fertilizers (EC, 2007). In terms of emissions, some authors have shown that conventional systems have lower CF than organic ones under Mediterranean conditions (Buratti et al., 2017; Pirlo and Lolli, 2019), but globally there is a lack of a consistent pattern (Poore and Nemecek, 2018; Clark and Tilman, 2017). In any case, the differences between organic and conventional CF are generally lower in extensive livestock systems (Horrillo et al., 2020) even if the organic systems have reduced performances. Given this knowledge gap, the increasing presence of organic farms and animals year by year in Europe, and particularly in Spain (MAPA, 2021) makes necessary to clarify the different levels of GHG emissions of organic and conventional systems.

It is composed of herbaceous pastures, some shrubs, scattered oak trees (mostly holm and cork oaks, 20-40 trees ha and sometimes extensive crops. The main livestock types are bovine, ovine and porcine, but there are other marginal animal products such as honey or game (Rodríguez-Estévez et al., 2012); so its use results in a great diversity of products such as meat, wool, milk, some annual crops, wood and cork (Escribano et al., 2016). Its climate is Mediterranean, which is semiarid, and has soils poor in nutrients and prone to erosion (Moreno and Pulido, 2009).

towards beef production has been promoted for various reasons, such as greater profitability (Hartel and Plieninger, 2014), or more subsidies from the European Common Agrarian Policy for cattle than for sheep (Moreno and Pulido, 2009). Such changes are hampering the oaks' natural replacement and the quality of the soil and pastures (Carmona et al., 2013; Moreno and Pulido, 2009).

10: The functional unit used in this study is 1 kg of live weight (LW) of calf at the end of the fattening period, which corresponds to an average LW of 500 kg and 14 months of age for the farms studied.

The system boundaries were "cradle to farm gate", including pre- farm and on farm emissions associated to feeding, electricity, fuel, and machinery use and animal linked emissions (enteric fermentation, manure management and manure deposition in soils) (Fig. 1). In addi- tion, the C sequestration, both in its trees and in its soils, is included in the systems.
11: Data from 15 extensive suckler cattle farms (6 organically managed (ORG) and 9 conventionally managed (CONV)), 4 of these with facilities for fattening calves were collected in this study; besides that, data from 2 specialised fattening farms that fattened calves raised in other suckler farms were also collected. The farms were located in the dehesa agro- ecosystem (Fig. 2) belonging to two Spanish regions (Andalucia and Extremadura) and 4 provinces (Cordoba, Seville, Caceres and Badajoz). The fattening farms data were used to model the fattening period in order to perform the LCA for those farms without fattening period in the dehesa pastures, which sell calves at weaning age. The data collection was performed in 2019. Most of the studied cattle farms had multispe- cies production (cows, sheep, goats, pigs, bees ...).

The data were obtained by direct interviews with farmers. The sur- veys included questions about different aspects of farm facilities, farm location (Fig. 3), animal handling, herd structure, animal feeding, external inputs, and productive data (Table 1). Some information was not provided directly by farmers and was obtained as follows: the number of trees per ha was calculated with samples of 1 ha of the different zones of the farm through a GIS map (MAPAMA, 2021), and the amount of pasture grazed by the animals was calculated based on the intake of dry matter and the contribution of external feed inputs.
The Livestock Units (LU) have been calculated according to MARM (2010) and have been used to measure the SR (Table S1).

The farms were located in the dehesa agro- ecosystem (Fig. 2) belonging to two Spanish regions (Andalucia and Extremadura) and 4 provinces (Cordoba, Seville, Caceres and Badajoz). 13: The cattle breeding system on the dehesa farms studied is focused on the production of weaned calves. The main components of herds are cows with calves and a small number of bulls. The production is oriented to meat production, and there are no indoor facilities, only outdoor pens for occasional handling and veterinary procedures.

The average calving time on the bovine farms in the dehesa is 6 months of life and 230 kg of LW. At this point there are 2 ways to follow to finish calves: to fatten the animals in the same facilities or fatten them on other farm facilities until they weigh around 500 kgLW at 14 months

of life. Animal diet on dehesa farms is not only based on grazing grass but also browsing bushes and Quercus fodder and acorns; however, the use of external feed inputs is widespread for cattle; especially in summer and early autumn, when there is a lack of fodder or dry grass quality is not

good enough to supply animal necessities of energy, and all year around when the SR is over the carrying capacity. In pasture-based systems there is no management of animal excretion; urine and dung are directly deposited in the soil.

17: <header>2.2. Carbon footprint calculation </header>

2.2.1. Estimation of emissions The emissions calculated were: CH4 from enteric fermentation, CH4 and N2O from manure management, N2O from excreta deposition in soils, external feed supply, and energy and machinery use. All the
emissions were expressed as kg CO2 equivalent (CO2 eq) using the climate feedbacks-corrected global warming potentials of 34 for CH4 and 298 for N2O (IPCC, 2013).

CH4emissions from enteric fermentation were estimated with Tier 2 equations from the 2019 revision of the IPCC guidelines (IPCC et al., 2019), based on the gross energy of feed (Equation 1). The feed com- positions (Table S2) were provided by the main feed producer of the region and the nutritional values to make the calculations were taken from FEDNA (FEDNA, 2011a,b).

where EF refers to the emission factor (kg CH4 head yr-1), GE to gross
energy intake (MJ head-¹ day-1), and Ym to the CH4conversion factor (% of gross energy in feed converted to CH4).

23: <header>2.2.2. Carbon sequestration </header>

The quantification of the soil C sequestration rates in dehesa systems were based on the comparison of soil C stock (30 cm depth) directly measured in 2018 with stocks measured and reported by the literature during an average of 22 years before on dehesa farms. Besides that, a review was performed to compile studies from the scientific and grey literature that included: 1) sampling points in dehesas of the study area finely geo-referenced; 2) profiles of at least 30 cm depth (separated in 0-5, 5-10 and 10-30 cm); 3) description of soils' physico-chemical characteristics (bulk density, pH, soil texture fractions, N, CEC, K, Mg, Ca); 4) sampling date at least 10 years prior to our sampling year. A total of 110 points with data with a mean age of 22 years (ranging from 12 to 59), were selected to make the measurements again: 55 geo-points were extracted from Pulido-Fernández et al. (2015); 26 geo-points belonging to the soil database generated in the framework of the ICP-Forest Pro- gramme (International Cooperation Programmes) and published by Montoya and López-Arias (1997); 30 geo-points belonging to the soil database generated in the framework of the Red CARBOSOL Project and published by Llorente et al. (2018).
The C stock of the soils was estimated as the product of the soil organic carbon (SOC), its bulk density (BD) and the thickness of the soil profile considered, according to Equation 9
SOCS = SOC .BDi.T Equation 9

where SOC refers to the C concentration in the soil sample, BD to bulk density (g cm-3), and T to layer thickness (30 cm, according to EU directive). The estimation of the soil C sequestration rate was made from the difference in C stocks over time (ASOCstock) in proportion to the elapsed time, as shown in Equation 10.
Equation 10
where SOCst refers to SOC stock per unit, t1 to the year of first sam- pling, and t2 to the year of second sampling.

The C sequestration of the vegetal biomass was estimated based on the National Forest Inventory (NFI) data (MAPA, 2020), making the comparison between the C stock described in the second (1986-1996) and third editions (1997-2007), with a 10 year gap. 3823 dehesa plots were assessed, with the following criteria: a) points that were sampled in both NFI2 and NFI3; b) main tree species (>50%) represented by Quercus sp.; c) tree density below 80 trees ha-1. Criteria b) and c) were established following the definition of dehesa systems of Pulido et al. (2010). NFIs comparison and management was done using Basifor 2.0 software (Bravo Oviedo et al., 2004).

Structural measurements of NFI2 and NFI3 for all 3823 selected plots were converted to biomass C densities by using allometric equations. IPCC (2006) guide equations for above and belowground tree biomass was used, and specific Biomass Expansion Factor (BEF), Root-to-Shoot Ratio (R), and Carbon Fraction for each species were used when avail- able in the Montero et al. (2005) database.
Tree biomass stock (including above and belowground) per unit of area (Mg dry matterha-1 was estimated according to Equation 11: Bha = VOB .BEF (1 Equation 11
where VOB refers to volume over bark (m³ ha BEF to biomass expansion factor, and R to ratio of aboveground oven-dry biomass of tree to belowground oven-dry biomass.
The CB change rate (ACB) per unit area and time (Mg equivalent to the C sequestration rate, was estimated according to Equation 12
(BhaCF2) - (BhaCF1) ACB Equation 12
where CF refers to specific C fraction for each species, CB to C biomass stock per unit area, t1 to the year reported in IFN2, and t2 to the year reported in IFN3.

Another process included in this study is CH4 fluxes from the soil, which are usually negative in pasture-based systems. It has been included using the data that Shvaleva et al. (2015) found specifically in dehesa agroecosystems.
2.2.3. Allocation

Following de Vries and de Boer (2010), economic allocation (Table S 4) was used for the indistinguishable emissions such as machinery use, electricity consumption or C sequestration, because there were more than one livestock species on most farms.
2.3. Statistical analysis

The descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation) have been calculated to describe the basic features of the emissions of both farming systems (CONV and ORG). The differences between both systems were assessed using one-way ANOVA for p 0.05.
2.4. Sensitivity analysis

A sensitivity analysis was carried out to test the effect of the decision process in this study (Table 2). The applying of IPCC 2007 conversion factor (25 CO2eq) for the CH4 (IPCC, 2007) and the IPCC, 2013 global warming potential without the climate feedback-corrected (28 and 265 CO2eq) for CH4 and N2O respectively (IPCC, 2013) instead of the climate feedback-corrected IPCC 2013 global warming potential values were tested individually. The effect of not including C sequestration in the CF, not differentiating between organic and conventional feed inputs emissions factor, the use of default Tier 1 EFs and a scenario where the nutritional differences between autumn-winter pastures and early summer pastures were also tested.
Table 2
Scenarios for the sensitivity analysis.

Name|GWP CH4|GWP N2O|C seq|Different EF of feed in ORG/CONV|Other a. GWP CH4 25|25|298|Yes|Yes| b. GWP without climate feedbacks|28|265|Yes|Yes| c. No differences ORG/CONV|34|298|Yes|No| d. C seq not included|34|298|No|Yes| e. Tier 1|34|298|Yes|Yes|Default EF for enteric CH4 manure management and managed soil emissions. f. Seasonal differences in nutritional composition|34|298|Yes|Yes|Differences between nutritional composition in pastures between seasons

The applying of IPCC 2007 conversion factor (25 CO2eq) for the CH4 (IPCC, 2007) and the IPCC, 2013 global warming potential without the climate feedback-corrected (28 and 265 CO2eq) for CH4 and N2O respectively (IPCC, 2013) instead of the climate feedback-corrected IPCC 2013 global warming potential values were tested individually. The effect of not including C sequestration in the CF, not differentiating between organic and conventional feed inputs emissions factor, the use of default Tier 1 EFs and a scenario where the nutritional differences between autumn-winter pastures and early summer pastures were also tested.

Scenarios for the sensitivity analysis.

C seq not included|34|298|No|Yes| e. Tier 1|34|298|Yes|Yes|Default EF for enteric CH4 manure management and managed soil emissions. f. Seasonal differences in nutritional composition|34|298|Yes|Yes|Differences between nutritional composition in pastures between seasons
28: <header>2.2.2. Carbon sequestration </header>

The quantification of the soil C sequestration rates in dehesa systems were based on the comparison of soil C stock (30 cm depth) directly measured in 2018 with stocks measured and reported by the literature during an average of 22 years before on dehesa farms. Besides that, a review was performed to compile studies from the scientific and grey literature that included: 1) sampling points in dehesas of the study area finely geo-referenced; 2) profiles of at least 30 cm depth (separated in 0-5, 5-10 and 10-30 cm); 3) description of soils' physico-chemical characteristics (bulk density, pH, soil texture fractions, N, CEC, K, Mg, Ca); 4) sampling date at least 10 years prior to our sampling year. A total of 110 points with data with a mean age of 22 years (ranging from 12 to 59), were selected to make the measurements again: 55 geo-points were extracted from Pulido-Fernández et al. (2015); 26 geo-points belonging to the soil database generated in the framework of the ICP-Forest Pro- gramme (International Cooperation Programmes) and published by Montoya and López-Arias (1997); 30 geo-points belonging to the soil database generated in the framework of the Red CARBOSOL Project and published by Llorente et al. (2018).
The C stock of the soils was estimated as the product of the soil organic carbon (SOC), its bulk density (BD) and the thickness of the soil profile considered, according to Equation 9
SOCS = SOC .BDi.T Equation 9

where SOC refers to the C concentration in the soil sample, BD to bulk density (g cm-3), and T to layer thickness (30 cm, according to EU directive). The estimation of the soil C sequestration rate was made from the difference in C stocks over time (ASOCstock) in proportion to the elapsed time, as shown in Equation 10.
Equation 10
where SOCst refers to SOC stock per unit, t1 to the year of first sam- pling, and t2 to the year of second sampling.

The C sequestration of the vegetal biomass was estimated based on the National Forest Inventory (NFI) data (MAPA, 2020), making the comparison between the C stock described in the second (1986-1996) and third editions (1997-2007), with a 10 year gap. 3823 dehesa plots were assessed, with the following criteria: a) points that were sampled in both NFI2 and NFI3; b) main tree species (>50%) represented by Quercus sp.; c) tree density below 80 trees ha-1. Criteria b) and c) were established following the definition of dehesa systems of Pulido et al. (2010). NFIs comparison and management was done using Basifor 2.0 software (Bravo Oviedo et al., 2004).

Structural measurements of NFI2 and NFI3 for all 3823 selected plots were converted to biomass C densities by using allometric equations. IPCC (2006) guide equations for above and belowground tree biomass was used, and specific Biomass Expansion Factor (BEF), Root-to-Shoot Ratio (R), and Carbon Fraction for each species were used when avail- able in the Montero et al. (2005) database.
Tree biomass stock (including above and belowground) per unit of area (Mg dry matterha-1 was estimated according to Equation 11: Bha = VOB .BEF (1 Equation 11
where VOB refers to volume over bark (m³ ha BEF to biomass expansion factor, and R to ratio of aboveground oven-dry biomass of tree to belowground oven-dry biomass.
The CB change rate (ACB) per unit area and time (Mg equivalent to the C sequestration rate, was estimated according to Equation 12
(BhaCF2) - (BhaCF1) ACB Equation 12
where CF refers to specific C fraction for each species, CB to C biomass stock per unit area, t1 to the year reported in IFN2, and t2 to the year reported in IFN3.

Another process included in this study is CH4 fluxes from the soil, which are usually negative in pasture-based systems. It has been included using the data that Shvaleva et al. (2015) found specifically in dehesa agroecosystems.
2.2.3. Allocation

Following de Vries and de Boer (2010), economic allocation (Table S 4) was used for the indistinguishable emissions such as machinery use, electricity consumption or C sequestration, because there were more than one livestock species on most farms.
2.3. Statistical analysis

The descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation) have been calculated to describe the basic features of the emissions of both farming systems (CONV and ORG). The differences between both systems were assessed using one-way ANOVA for p 0.05.
2.4. Sensitivity analysis

A sensitivity analysis was carried out to test the effect of the decision process in this study (Table 2). The applying of IPCC 2007 conversion factor (25 CO2eq) for the CH4 (IPCC, 2007) and the IPCC, 2013 global warming potential without the climate feedback-corrected (28 and 265 CO2eq) for CH4 and N2O respectively (IPCC, 2013) instead of the climate feedback-corrected IPCC 2013 global warming potential values were tested individually. The effect of not including C sequestration in the CF, not differentiating between organic and conventional feed inputs emissions factor, the use of default Tier 1 EFs and a scenario where the nutritional differences between autumn-winter pastures and early summer pastures were also tested.

29: The technical characterisation of the studied farms is shown in Table 3, the averages weights of animals are shown in Table S 5. Cows and lactating calves are in pastures 100% of the time, following the generalised practice on dehesa farms, while calves are raised in pens during the fattening phase. The reproduction performance is 0.8 + 0.1 calf year with an average ratio of 31.5 + 11.9 cows bull-1

Supplementary food is provided to the grazing animals at some time of the year on all farms; mainly in summer and early autumn when the pastures are parched and scarce. The dehesa farms studied have a wide range of extension with an average of 542.7 ha, ranging from 20 to 3050 ha. The average SR in the sample is 0.75 1 0.31 LU ha Six of the farms (40%) were certified as organic, and the differences with the conven- tional ones (9) are mainly related to the tree density, the herd size and the lower external feed supply (Table 3).
The calves are slaughtered with an average LW and age of 495.6 kg and 14.3 months.
3.2.1. Greenhouse gas emissions and carbon sequestration

The average GHG emissions in the farms studied were 20 + 5.96 kg COeq KgLw. Most of the emissions were related to enteric fermentation (an average of 11.82 + 2.8 kg CO2eq kgLw), and the external inputs was the second source of emissions with 6.45 + 3.72 kg CO2eq kglw. The other sources of emissions were 1.73 + 0.63 kg CO2eq kgLw

The current mean stock of soil organic C (0-30 cm depth) of the dehesa farms studied was 77.6 + 8.4 t C.ha with great variability between sites. For the same soil profile depth, SOC sequestration rate averaged 0.91 + 0.26 t C.ha~1.year-1. The tree biomass C stock was 7.25 + 0.89 C ha -Superscript(1), with also a great variability between sites. The mean C sequestration rate of trees in dehesa systems amounted to 0.07 + 0.10 t C.ha~1-year-1. In summary, soils and tree biomass together added up to an average sink of 3.570 t CO2-ha-1-year™ which means an average C sequestration of 13.52 + 19.09 kg CO2eq kgLw.

Carbon sequestration inclusion balances emissions by an average of 68% (95% and 54% in ORG and CONV farms, respectively), the average CF of these extensive cattle livestock farms being 6.41 + 21.46 kg CO2eq kglw. In some cases, C sequestration compensates all GHG emissions and results in negative CF (Fig. 6).
Table 3 Description of the dehesa cattle farms.
* indicates statistical differences (p < 0.05) between organic and conventional; +LU livestock unit (see equivalences in Table S1).

38: The average CF of ORG farms is 0.9 + 22.99 kg CO2eq kg while for CONV ones is 10.11 + 20.91 kg CO2eq kglw. The distribution of emis- sions differs between ORG and CONV management (Fig. 4). Most of the emissions were originated in the farms; however, external inputs represent a larger percentage of the final emissions in the CONV farms (37%), which is higher than these emissions source in ORG farms (23%).

The ORG production had a CF 91% lower on average than the CONV production (Fig. 5i), but those differences were not significant. Signifi- cant differences between systems were only found for the emissions linked to feed inputs (Fig. 5e).

The CF of calves from 2 out 15 of the farms had a negative balance because their C sequestration surpasses the emissions (Fig. 6). The farms with the highest emissions by functional unit were those with a low productivity near to 0.5 calf cow year (Fig. 6a).
Fig. 4. Carbon Footprint of organic (ORG) and conventional (CONV) for cattle (kg CO2 eqkgLw) on dehesa farms.

40: The GWP value election can reduce or increase the GHG emissions calculations. In the case of extensive livestock, in which the enteric fermentation plays a principal role in total emission, the use of a different CH4 conversion factor has a great impact on final emission. In this study, the results with 34 as conversion factor for CH4are twice as high as 25 (IPCC, 2007); and if the CH4 conversion factor were 28 (IPCC, 2013) the final CF would be 35% lower than with the values chosen in this study.

Other key assumptions have been tested to compare the differences in CF. For example, the decision of using different emission factor for organic and conventional feed raw materials or the differentiation of the nutritional values of pastures in different seasons, both slightly increase the final CF of the farms studied. The last possibility considered was the tier 1 emission factor for cattle, instead of the calculations according to tier 2 equations of the IPCC. The lack of presence of specific data could lead to some differences in the calculation of the livestock emissions as is shown in Fig. 7, in which the calculation of emissions with the decisions explained in this study is 31% lower than the CF obtained applying the default values.

It is evident that the decisions of using different emissions factors or some key assumptions (as the differentiation between organic or con- ventional feed inputs) affects the final CF (Fig. 7). In all scenarios, the average CF for ORG farms was lower than in the CONV ones in 91, 134, 117, 86, 21, 81 and 73% in each scenario (a to f, Table 2) respectively, but no significant differences exist between management in any scenario.

43: In consonance with other LCA studies about beef production (De Vries et al., 2015; McAuliffe et al., 2018), CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation were the single greatest source of GHG emissions inde- pendently of pasture management strategies. Enteric CH4 emissions are dependent on type of diet, feed intake and its digestibility (Eldesouky et al., 2018). The roughage diet has more dry matter and less digestible energy, producing more CH4 emissions than a concentrate-based diet with the same nutritional value (Casey and Holden, 2006; Nguyen et al., 2010). This type of diet has also been associated with higher overall GHG emissions than concentrate-based diets (De Vries et al., 2015; McAuliffe et al., 2018; Nguyen et al., 2010; Pelletier et al., 2010). Be- sides the effect on enteric CH4, the growth rate of animals is lower on

roughage-based systems, and they have a longer finishing time or lower finishing weight, depending on the quality of forage, which have a sig- nificant impact on the emissions per kg LW (Dick et al., 2015). On average, the results of this study show something similar, as the calves finished on the dehesa farms produce more GHG than feedlot-finished ones (Fig. S1), but those results are not conclusive, because there is a large variation between farms; some of the fattened calves finished on dehesa had lower emissions than some of the feedlot-finished ones, and the pasture-finished systems sample is very small. Besides that, tannins and plant secondary metabolites, which are common in the diet of cows in oak-based dehesa farms (de Oliveira et al., 2013), have the potential to reduce enteric CH4 emissions (Ku-Vera et al., 2020; Min et al., 2020), but this effect is not included in the tier 2 IPCC models (IPCC et al., 2019) used for the estimation of enteric CH4 emissions in this study. On the other hand, the concerns over anti-nutritional effects of these compounds, and the low availability of in
Fig. 5. GHG emissions by kg of live weight at fattening farm gate in con- ventional (CONV) and organic (ORG) dehesa cattle farms.

*Significant differences (p 0.05). The whiskers represent the zero (0% percentile) and the fourth quartile (100% percentile), the box is repre- sented between the first (25% percen- tile) and third quartile (75% percentile), the horizontal bar in the box represents the median (50% percentile) and the X represents the mean. The dots are the outliers.

Fig. 6. Relationship between (a) GHG emissions and cow productivity, (b) carbon footprint and cow productivity, (c) GHG emissions and stocking rate, and (d) carbon footprint and stocking rate in dehesa farms. (CONV: conventional; ORG:organic).

vivo studies on the effect of tannins and secondary metabolites on enteric CH4 emissions (Dhanasekaran et al., 2020), suggest the need for more research in this field. In particular, no effect on enteric CH4 emissions was found with the dietary addition of tannins from Quercus

Fig. 7. Comparison of the effects of the different scenarios of calculations for carbon footprint on the dehesa cattle farms. Scenario base cases, a. and b. correspond to different conversion factors for CH4 (34, 25, or 28) and N2O (298 or 265); C. not differ- entiating the external feed inputs EF between CONV and ORG; d. not including C sequestration; e. using Tier 1 calculations, and f. including the variations in nutritional composition in pastures thought the sea- sons (ORG: organic; CONV: conventional) (For a detailed description of the scenarios a to f see Table 1).
robur (Focant et al., 2019), but there are no studies including direct measurements of enteric CH4 emissions from cows grazing on dehesa pastures.
Considering the variability between farms, the estimated CF values

in this study are similar to those of other studies carried in dehesa agroecosystems. Eldesouky et al. (2018) show 6.34 CO2 eq KgLW for conventional fattened calves and 12.35 CO2 eq kglwfor sucklers, and Horrillo et al. (2020) show 4.48 eq kglw for organic fattened calves, all in only one sampled farm. These previous studies, based on assess- ments of single farms, also included C sequestration in the emissions balance, based on rough estimations; on the contrary, in the present study, C sequestration was based on field measurements, resulting in a larger contribution of this process to the reduction of the total CF. In the present study, there is a very wide variability (more than 100%) be- tween farms, even including some farms with negative emission bal- ances, which shows how important is to have a representative sample of the systems studied to obtain sound results and conclusions. Other pasture-based systems studied shown higher CF than cattle production in the dehesa agroecosystem (Buratti et al., 2017; Pelletier et al., 2010), even if these previous studies used lower conversion factors for CH4. The differences between the present study and Buratti et al. (2017) could be explained because only the C sequestration linked to feed production was accounted for and not the C sequestration linked to the system. In the case of Pelletier et al. (2010) the main difference is related to the lower amount of C sequestered ha year

Several key assumptions must be made for the calculations of GHG emissions following the IPCC guidelines (Lupo et al., 2013), and the LCA method, which hinder the comparisons between different studies (De Vries et al., 2015). The different conversion factors for GHG emissions chosen in other studies add other difficulty to make comparisons be- tween results. Most of the previous studies have used 25 and 298 as GWP values respectively for CH4 and N2O (Buratti et al., 2017; Eldesouky et al., 2018; Horrillo et al., 2020), but others have used different con- version factors: as 22 (Dick et al., 2015), 28 (McAuliffe et al., 2018) or 21 for CH4, and 310 (Casey and Holden, 2006a, b) or 265 (McAuliffe et al., 2018) for N20.

Considering the variability between farms, the estimated CF values

in this study are similar to those of other studies carried in dehesa agroecosystems. Eldesouky et al. (2018) show 6.34 CO2 eq KgLW for conventional fattened calves and 12.35 CO2 eq kglwfor sucklers, and Horrillo et al. (2020) show 4.48 eq kglw for organic fattened calves, all in only one sampled farm. These previous studies, based on assess- ments of single farms, also included C sequestration in the emissions balance, based on rough estimations; on the contrary, in the present study, C sequestration was based on field measurements, resulting in a larger contribution of this process to the reduction of the total CF. In the present study, there is a very wide variability (more than 100%) be- tween farms, even including some farms with negative emission bal- ances, which shows how important is to have a representative sample of the systems studied to obtain sound results and conclusions. Other pasture-based systems studied shown higher CF than cattle production in the dehesa agroecosystem (Buratti et al., 2017; Pelletier et al., 2010), even if these previous studies used lower conversion factors for CH4. The differences between the present study and Buratti et al. (2017) could be explained because only the C sequestration linked to feed production was accounted for and not the C sequestration linked to the system. In the case of Pelletier et al. (2010) the main difference is related to the lower amount of C sequestered ha year

Several key assumptions must be made for the calculations of GHG emissions following the IPCC guidelines (Lupo et al., 2013), and the LCA method, which hinder the comparisons between different studies (De Vries et al., 2015). The different conversion factors for GHG emissions chosen in other studies add other difficulty to make comparisons be- tween results. Most of the previous studies have used 25 and 298 as GWP values respectively for CH4 and N2O (Buratti et al., 2017; Eldesouky et al., 2018; Horrillo et al., 2020), but others have used different con- version factors: as 22 (Dick et al., 2015), 28 (McAuliffe et al., 2018) or 21 for CH4, and 310 (Casey and Holden, 2006a, b) or 265 (McAuliffe et al., 2018) for N20.
48: In consonance with other LCA studies about beef production (De Vries et al., 2015; McAuliffe et al., 2018), CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation were the single greatest source of GHG emissions inde- pendently of pasture management strategies. Enteric CH4 emissions are dependent on type of diet, feed intake and its digestibility (Eldesouky et al., 2018). The roughage diet has more dry matter and less digestible energy, producing more CH4 emissions than a concentrate-based diet with the same nutritional value (Casey and Holden, 2006; Nguyen et al., 2010). This type of diet has also been associated with higher overall GHG emissions than concentrate-based diets (De Vries et al., 2015; McAuliffe et al., 2018; Nguyen et al., 2010; Pelletier et al., 2010). Be- sides the effect on enteric CH4, the growth rate of animals is lower on

roughage-based systems, and they have a longer finishing time or lower finishing weight, depending on the quality of forage, which have a sig- nificant impact on the emissions per kg LW (Dick et al., 2015). On average, the results of this study show something similar, as the calves finished on the dehesa farms produce more GHG than feedlot-finished ones (Fig. S1), but those results are not conclusive, because there is a large variation between farms; some of the fattened calves finished on dehesa had lower emissions than some of the feedlot-finished ones, and the pasture-finished systems sample is very small. Besides that, tannins and plant secondary metabolites, which are common in the diet of cows in oak-based dehesa farms (de Oliveira et al., 2013), have the potential to reduce enteric CH4 emissions (Ku-Vera et al., 2020; Min et al., 2020), but this effect is not included in the tier 2 IPCC models (IPCC et al., 2019) used for the estimation of enteric CH4 emissions in this study. On the other hand, the concerns over anti-nutritional effects of these compounds, and the low availability of in

The different conversion factors for GHG emissions chosen in other studies add other difficulty to make comparisons be- tween results. Most of the previous studies have used 25 and 298 as GWP values respectively for CH4 and N2O (Buratti et al., 2017; Eldesouky et al., 2018; Horrillo et al., 2020), but others have used different con- version factors: as 22 (Dick et al., 2015), 28 (McAuliffe et al., 2018) or 21 for CH4, and 310 (Casey and Holden, 2006a, b) or 265 (McAuliffe et al., 2018) for N20.
50: The reduction for the CF for ORG management obtained in this study is larger than that obtained by De Vries et al. (2015) where the CF per unit of product was 7% lower in organic systems compared with non-organic ones. The difference in CF between the two management systems was partially explained by a lower use, of external inputs of concentrate feeds with raw materials from far regions and other coun- tries in ORG systems (Casey and Holden, 2006). The current results showed a positive correlation between CF and the SR on dehesa cattle farms (Fig. 7), contrary to previous studies (Bragaglio et al., 2018; Eldesouky et al., 2018; Pelletier et al., 2010). This trend was significant even without the C sequestration (Fig. 7c), but it was clearer when it was included (Fig. 7d).

The average SR of farms studied was in consonance with the average SR of 0.73 LU ha reported for the dehesa system by (Maroto-Molina et al., 2018), but over the SR range (0.37-0.49 LU ha-1 reported by many other authors (e.g., (Díaz- Gaona et al., 2014a; Escribano et al., 2014). The carrying capacity, defined as the number of LU on a specific surface that is sustained over time, has been estimated at 0.28-0.36 LU ha for dehesa agroecosystems (Díaz- Gaona et al., 2014a), with a wider range of 0.2-0.6 LU ha reported by (Olea and San Miguel-Ayanz, 2006). The upgrade of stoking rates in the extensive systems, and specifically in the dehesa, has been one effect of the Eu- ropean Common Agricultural Policy (CAP), whose reference limit (1.4 (EC, 2003) is really high in comparison with the carrying ca- pacity of the dehesa system. This, together with the subsidies per reproductive cow, led to a change in the predominant livestock species and an increase in the SR on the dehesa farms (Díaz- Gaona et al., 2014a; Moreno and Pulido, 2009). While the use of external feed was residual in the traditional dehesa system, nowadays it is widespread (Mar- oto-Molina et al., 2018). However, organic farms tend to maintain a reduced and more sustainable SR, from 0.28 to 0.36 LU ha on dehesas

located in natural protected areas (Díaz- Gaona et al., 2014b). In the current study, the average CF of the beef produced with SR below the upper boundary of the carrying capacity estimated by Díaz-Gaona et al. (2014b), of 0.36 LU was -26.7 kg CO2eq kglw, whereas it aver- aged 14.71 kg CO2eq kgLw on farms with SR above that value. These results suggest that adjusting SR to the carrying capacity could be an effective climate change mitigation strategy on the dehesa.

Most external inputs in extensive systems are related to the feed supply, but the amount of feed is not very high compared with intensive systems in which the whole feed ration is external in most cases. Even if the inclusion of concentrate feed in animal production increases the animal productivity and reduces on-farm emissions, when the concen- trate feed production emissions are included, final GHG emissions may not decrease in intensive systems (Opio et al., 2013). On the farms studied, the use of concentrate feed inputs is higher on CONV farms, and this is reflected in associated emissions (Fig. 5e).

Carbon sequestration can be defined as the uptake of C-containing substances and, in particular, atmospheric CO2 into another reservoir with a longer residence time (IPCC, 2007). Given that sequestration on the dehesa is mainly dependent on area, the less livestock density the more sequestration per functional unit, reducing the final CF, and on some farms, it results in a negative CF for the calf product. This result contrasts with the widely accepted view that strategies to mitigate the GHG emission of beef production should focus on productive upgrading and intensification to reduce the CF (Dick et al., 2015). In this line, previous studies have identified a clear relationship between intensifi- cation and lower CF (Buratti et al., 2017; Eldesouky et al., 2018), due to the higher efficiency of intensive systems (Eldesouky et al., 2018). Therefore, the results of the current study suggest that the conclusions of previous studies regarding the role of intensification in the reduction of the CF only considered an increase of productivity in the whole system. This increase of productivity decreases negative externalities per func- tional unit. However, due to some limitations, the previous studies did not consider the positives externalities, such as C sequestration in extensive and agrosilvopastoral systems; which considerably reduces the final CF (Balmford et al., 2018).

Livestock intensification based on a more efficient use of forage re- sources of the dehesa farms could also increase the C sequestration and reduce CF of cattle (Soussana and Lemaire, 2014; Tedeschi et al., 2015; Tittonell et al., 2016). A well reported example of this sustainable intensification that strengthens the soil C sequestration and reduces the CF of dehesa farms is the overseeding of legume-rich pastures (Morais et al., 2018; Moreno et al., 2021).

Most external inputs in extensive systems are related to the feed supply, but the amount of feed is not very high compared with intensive systems in which the whole feed ration is external in most cases. Even if the inclusion of concentrate feed in animal production increases the animal productivity and reduces on-farm emissions, when the concen- trate feed production emissions are included, final GHG emissions may not decrease in intensive systems (Opio et al., 2013). On the farms studied, the use of concentrate feed inputs is higher on CONV farms, and this is reflected in associated emissions (Fig. 5e).

Carbon sequestration can be defined as the uptake of C-containing substances and, in particular, atmospheric CO2 into another reservoir with a longer residence time (IPCC, 2007). Given that sequestration on the dehesa is mainly dependent on area, the less livestock density the more sequestration per functional unit, reducing the final CF, and on some farms, it results in a negative CF for the calf product. This result contrasts with the widely accepted view that strategies to mitigate the GHG emission of beef production should focus on productive upgrading and intensification to reduce the CF (Dick et al., 2015). In this line, previous studies have identified a clear relationship between intensifi- cation and lower CF (Buratti et al., 2017; Eldesouky et al., 2018), due to the higher efficiency of intensive systems (Eldesouky et al., 2018). Therefore, the results of the current study suggest that the conclusions of previous studies regarding the role of intensification in the reduction of the CF only considered an increase of productivity in the whole system. This increase of productivity decreases negative externalities per func- tional unit. However, due to some limitations, the previous studies did not consider the positives externalities, such as C sequestration in extensive and agrosilvopastoral systems; which considerably reduces the final CF (Balmford et al., 2018).

Livestock intensification based on a more efficient use of forage re- sources of the dehesa farms could also increase the C sequestration and reduce CF of cattle (Soussana and Lemaire, 2014; Tedeschi et al., 2015; Tittonell et al., 2016). A well reported example of this sustainable intensification that strengthens the soil C sequestration and reduces the CF of dehesa farms is the overseeding of legume-rich pastures (Morais et al., 2018; Moreno et al., 2021).
54: The reduction for the CF for ORG management obtained in this study is larger than that obtained by De Vries et al. (2015) where the CF per unit of product was 7% lower in organic systems compared with non-organic ones. The difference in CF between the two management systems was partially explained by a lower use, of external inputs of concentrate feeds with raw materials from far regions and other coun- tries in ORG systems (Casey and Holden, 2006). The current results showed a positive correlation between CF and the SR on dehesa cattle farms (Fig. 7), contrary to previous studies (Bragaglio et al., 2018; Eldesouky et al., 2018; Pelletier et al., 2010). This trend was significant even without the C sequestration (Fig. 7c), but it was clearer when it was included (Fig. 7d).

The average SR of farms studied was in consonance with the average SR of 0.73 LU ha reported for the dehesa system by (Maroto-Molina et al., 2018), but over the SR range (0.37-0.49 LU ha-1 reported by many other authors (e.g., (Díaz- Gaona et al., 2014a; Escribano et al., 2014). The carrying capacity, defined as the number of LU on a specific surface that is sustained over time, has been estimated at 0.28-0.36 LU ha for dehesa agroecosystems (Díaz- Gaona et al., 2014a), with a wider range of 0.2-0.6 LU ha reported by (Olea and San Miguel-Ayanz, 2006). The upgrade of stoking rates in the extensive systems, and specifically in the dehesa, has been one effect of the Eu- ropean Common Agricultural Policy (CAP), whose reference limit (1.4 (EC, 2003) is really high in comparison with the carrying ca- pacity of the dehesa system. This, together with the subsidies per reproductive cow, led to a change in the predominant livestock species and an increase in the SR on the dehesa farms (Díaz- Gaona et al., 2014a; Moreno and Pulido, 2009). While the use of external feed was residual in the traditional dehesa system, nowadays it is widespread (Mar- oto-Molina et al., 2018). However, organic farms tend to maintain a reduced and more sustainable SR, from 0.28 to 0.36 LU ha on dehesas

located in natural protected areas (Díaz- Gaona et al., 2014b). In the current study, the average CF of the beef produced with SR below the upper boundary of the carrying capacity estimated by Díaz-Gaona et al. (2014b), of 0.36 LU was -26.7 kg CO2eq kglw, whereas it aver- aged 14.71 kg CO2eq kgLw on farms with SR above that value. These results suggest that adjusting SR to the carrying capacity could be an effective climate change mitigation strategy on the dehesa.

55: <header>4.3. The role of trees and soil in the carbon footprint </header>

C sequestration in agroforestry systems can partially slow the in- crease in atmospheric concentration of CO2 by storing SOC for millennia (Lorenz and Lal, 2014; Waldron et al., 2017). Agroforestry systems could store significant quantities of C in biomass and soils. The largest terrestrial pool of C is found in soils, with spatially variable but large annual C exchanges with the atmosphere. Moreover, SOC is the major determinant of soil quality, and it greatly influences the global C cycle and climate change.

Trees are the key differential element in agroforestry systems compared with other pasture lands (de Figueiredo et al., 2017). Mature trees could affect the SOC dynamics, mainly due to the contribution of their roots (Reyna-Bowen et al., 2020). In agroforestry systems, such as the dehesa agroecosystem, C stocks would be higher than in other pas- turelands without trees, due to growth of oaks biomass and the rein- forced soil C storage (Howlett et al., 2011), even if the C sequestration is affected by the species, the age, the historical management or the geographic location (Ruiz Peinado et al., 2017). However, it is important to note that C sequestration may be very site-specific (Seddaiu et al., 2018). Extensive livestock production is an interesting option to enhance soil C accumulation (Soussana et al., 2004), and it has been

found that grazing promotes belowground C allocation in Mediterra- nean and other semi-arid and pastures (Whitehead, 2020). However, the SR should be adapted to the productive capacity of the pastures where the animals live, because if this is above of the carrying capacity of the systems, a decrease in SOC stocks could occur (Abdalla et al., 2018) and tree recruitment is hindered. In this regard, it is also reported that high SR decreases soil fertility (Fernandez et al., 2008) and it can also reduce the potential of grasslands to capture CO2 by reducing aerial biomass (Frank et al., 2002) and increasing the soil compaction (Byrnes et al., 2018; Garnett et al., 2017).

The average C sequestration rates measured on the farms studied (ranging from 1.36 to 5.09 t CO2eq ha-1-year-1 is significantly higher than previous estimations included in other published LCA studies: 312.53 and 485.71 kg CO2eq in Eldesouky et al. (2018) and Horrillo et al. (2020) respectively. It is also important to indicate that a great variability of situations related to the C sequestration capability among farms has been found. Therefore, it can be concluded that it is necessary to carry out more studies on the management practices that allow an increase in C sequestration in these ecosystems. An improve- ment in the management of the soil and vegetation of dehesa pasture- lands could increase the current C sequestration rate of farms located within this agroecosystem and other grazed agroforestry systems, especially a reduction of SR to allow tree regeneration.

Most of the previous studies of pasture-based systems did not consider C sequestration. This deficiency is a handicap for extensive livestock farming differentiation, which could be a marketing opportu- nity based on its environmental contribution. Previous studies indicate that proper grazing strategies can increase the soil C storage (Schuman et al., 2002), and the inclusion of the sequestration in LCA calculations shows reduced GHG emissions of extensive systems, making these similar or even much lower than the non-extensive ones (Knudsen et al., 2019), although the intensive ones are more productive; what is more, the current LCA study shows that a negative CF is feasible.

It is necessary to point out that soil is not a permanent C sink, and that C saturation will be reached at some point, meaning that the soil would not be capable to sequester more C from the atmosphere. How- ever, C sequestration and the time when C saturation or a new steady estate are achieved by the soil are affected by many variables (Lal, 2018). In this sense, Lal et al. (2018) have estimated the C sequestration potential in different terrestrial ecosystems, showing that C saturation could only be realized with the application of the best management practices; however, there is a knowledge gap on C sequestration and equilibrium period, especially in agroforestry systems.

61: LCA is a widely employed methodology for the calculation of GHG emissions of the livestock farming sector, but it is important to justify and document all methodological choices, because these highly influ- ence the results, which make later comparisons difficult. Organic farming systems present numerically lower CF than conventional sys- items, mainly due to lower emissions associated to feed inputs. A direct relationship between extensification and reduced CF has been identified when C sequestration is included in the calculations. This study shows a major role of C sequestration in the GHG balance of dehesa cattle pro- duction, leading to negative net emissions in some cases. Therefore, the search for management strategies to increase soils potential for C sequestration beyond the supposed saturation point could be especially relevant.
